questions.stem: A data scientist wants to use a context manager pattern to ensure an MLflow run is properly closed even if an exception occurs during training. Which code pattern is correct?

options.text: run = mlflow.start_run(); model.fit(X, y); mlflow.end_run()
with mlflow.start_run() as run: model.fit(X, y); mlflow.log_metric('acc', acc)
mlflow.start_run(auto_close=True); model.fit(X, y)
mlflow.open_run(); model.fit(X, y); mlflow.close_run()

questions.explanation: B is correct because using mlflow.start_run() as a context manager (with statement) automatically calls mlflow.end_run() when the block exits, whether normally or due to an exception — ensuring the run is always finalized. A would leave the run open if an exception occurs between start_run() and end_run(). C uses a non-existent auto_close parameter. D uses non-existent mlflow.open_run() and mlflow.close_run() functions.
questions.reference: MLflow — mlflow.start_run() as context manager

questions.stem: A data scientist needs to log a pandas DataFrame as a CSV artifact to an MLflow run for later inspection. They have the DataFrame in memory as 'df'. What is the correct approach?

options.text: mlflow.log_param('dataframe', df.to_json())
df.to_csv('/tmp/results.csv'); mlflow.log_artifact('/tmp/results.csv')
mlflow.log_metric('dataframe', df)
mlflow.log_artifact(df, 'results.csv')

questions.explanation: B is correct because mlflow.log_artifact() requires a file path. The proper pattern is to first save the DataFrame to a local file and then call log_artifact() with the file path, which uploads the file to the run's artifact store. A truncates the DataFrame to a JSON string stored in params (params have character limits and are not designed for tabular data). C fails because log_metric only accepts numeric scalars. D fails because log_artifact does not accept Python objects directly — it requires a file path string.
questions.reference: MLflow — log_artifact() file path requirement

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to resume a previously created MLflow run to add additional metrics after finding new evaluation results. Which code pattern correctly resumes an existing run?

options.text: mlflow.set_experiment(run_id='abc123')
with mlflow.start_run(run_id='abc123'): mlflow.log_metric('new_metric', 0.95)
mlflow.resume_run('abc123'); mlflow.log_metric('new_metric', 0.95)
mlflow.create_run(run_id='abc123'); mlflow.log_metric('new_metric', 0.95)

questions.explanation: B is correct because mlflow.start_run(run_id='abc123') reopens an existing run by its ID, allowing additional logging to be appended to that run's metrics, params, and artifacts. The context manager ensures the run is properly closed after. A (set_experiment) sets the active experiment, not a run. C (resume_run) is not a valid MLflow function. D (create_run) would attempt to create a new run with a specified ID, not resume an existing one.
questions.reference: MLflow — mlflow.start_run(run_id=...) to resume an existing run

questions.stem:
A data scientist uses the following HyperOpt code but finds that SparkTrials is NOT distributing trials across workers as expected — all trials run on the driver. Select TWO likely causes.

```python
from hyperopt import fmin, tpe, Trials
best = fmin(objective, space, algo=tpe.suggest, max_evals=50, trials=Trials())
```

options.text: The code uses Trials() instead of SparkTrials() — Trials runs sequentially on the driver
The tpe.suggest algorithm is incompatible with SparkTrials
max_evals=50 exceeds the maximum supported by SparkTrials
SparkTrials was not imported — the code imports Trials from hyperopt but not SparkTrials

questions.explanation: A is correct because the code explicitly instantiates Trials() (the sequential driver-only trials object) rather than SparkTrials(parallelism=N), which is why no distribution occurs. D is correct because the import statement only imports Trials from hyperopt — SparkTrials must be imported separately with 'from hyperopt import SparkTrials' and then used as 'trials=SparkTrials(parallelism=4)'. B is wrong — tpe.suggest is fully compatible with SparkTrials. C is wrong — SparkTrials has no hard max_evals limit; max_evals is simply the total number of trials to run.
questions.reference: HyperOpt — SparkTrials vs Trials; correct import and instantiation

questions.stem: An ML engineer needs to log 100 step-wise loss values (one per training epoch) to MLflow so they can be plotted as a learning curve. Which is the correct approach?

options.text: mlflow.log_metric('loss', [0.9, 0.8, ..., 0.1]) — pass a list of all 100 values at once
mlflow.log_metrics({'loss_epoch_1': 0.9, 'loss_epoch_2': 0.8, ...}) — one key per epoch
Call mlflow.log_metric('loss', value, step=epoch) inside the training loop for each epoch
mlflow.log_artifact(losses_array, 'training_losses.npy')

questions.explanation: C is correct because mlflow.log_metric() supports an optional step parameter that associates the metric value with a training step/epoch. Calling it in a loop (for epoch in range(100): mlflow.log_metric('loss', loss, step=epoch)) creates a metric time series that the MLflow UI renders as a line chart. A fails because log_metric only accepts scalar values, not lists. B creates 100 separate metrics instead of a single time-series metric, losing the chart visualization. D would require saving to file first (log_artifact requires a file path), and a numpy array would not render as an interactive chart.
questions.reference: MLflow — mlflow.log_metric() with step parameter for time-series tracking

questions.stem: A data scientist runs mlflow.sklearn.autolog() before fitting a Pipeline that includes a StandardScaler followed by a LogisticRegression. What parameters are automatically logged?

options.text: Only the LogisticRegression parameters (C, penalty, solver) — preprocessing steps are ignored
Parameters from all pipeline steps, prefixed by step name (e.g., logisticregression__C, standardscaler__with_mean)
Only the Pipeline's own parameters (steps list) — individual step parameters require manual logging
No parameters are logged — autolog only logs metrics for Pipeline objects

questions.explanation: B is correct because mlflow.sklearn.autolog() introspects the entire sklearn Pipeline using get_params(deep=True), which returns all parameters from all pipeline steps with double-underscore prefixed names indicating their step (e.g., logisticregression__C=1.0, standardscaler__with_mean=True). This gives full reproducibility. A is wrong because the deep=True behavior captures all nested estimator parameters. C is wrong because deep=True traverses the entire pipeline. D is wrong because autolog does log parameters for Pipeline objects.
questions.reference: MLflow sklearn autolog — Pipeline parameter logging with deep=True

questions.stem: A team runs a large hyperparameter sweep and wants to find the run with the best validation AUC using only programmatic access (no MLflow UI). Which code snippet correctly retrieves the best run?

options.text: runs = mlflow.search_runs(experiment_names=['MyExperiment'], order_by=['metrics.val_auc DESC']); best = runs.iloc[0]
runs = mlflow.list_runs('MyExperiment'); best = max(runs, key=lambda r: r.metrics['val_auc'])
best = MlflowClient().get_best_run('MyExperiment', metric='val_auc')
runs = mlflow.search_runs(filter_string='metrics.val_auc = MAX(metrics.val_auc)')

questions.explanation: A is correct because mlflow.search_runs() returns results as a pandas DataFrame with order_by support, and order_by=['metrics.val_auc DESC'] sorts by validation AUC descending, so runs.iloc[0] is the best run. B uses mlflow.list_runs() which is not a valid top-level MLflow function. C uses MlflowClient().get_best_run() which does not exist. D uses SQL aggregate syntax (MAX) which is not supported in MLflow filter_string syntax — filter_string uses comparison operators, not aggregates.
questions.reference: mlflow.search_runs() — order_by for finding best run

questions.stem: An ML engineer logs a model with an inferred signature using the training data. In production, the model receives a request where one optional feature column is missing from the input DataFrame. The signature marks this column as required. What happens?

options.text: The model automatically imputes missing columns with the training mean
MLflow serving raises a schema enforcement error because the required column is absent
The column is silently ignored and predictions proceed without it
MLflow serving logs a warning but returns predictions using all other columns

questions.explanation: B is correct because when a model is served with schema enforcement (enabled by default when a signature is present), MLflow validates that all columns defined in the input schema are present in the request. A missing required column causes a schema enforcement exception (400 error from the serving endpoint). A is wrong — MLflow serving does not impute missing values. C is wrong — missing required columns are not silently ignored when schema enforcement is active. D is wrong — MLflow serving does not return partial predictions with a warning; it raises an error.
questions.reference: MLflow Model Serving — signature schema enforcement on missing columns

questions.stem: A data scientist has multiple MLflow experiments and wants to delete a completed experiment that is no longer needed. Which MlflowClient method removes the experiment?

options.text: MlflowClient().delete_experiment(experiment_id)
mlflow.remove_experiment(experiment_name)
MlflowClient().archive_experiment(experiment_id)
mlflow.set_experiment(experiment_id, status='deleted')

questions.explanation: A is correct because MlflowClient().delete_experiment(experiment_id) is the valid API method for soft-deleting an experiment. In MLflow's lifecycle model, deleted experiments are moved to a 'DELETED' state and can be restored — they are not immediately purged. B (mlflow.remove_experiment) is not a valid MLflow function. C (archive_experiment) does not exist as an MlflowClient method — there is no archive state for experiments (archiving applies to model versions). D is wrong because set_experiment() only sets the active experiment for logging, and status is not a valid parameter.
questions.reference: MlflowClient — delete_experiment() for experiment lifecycle management

questions.stem: A team is implementing MLOps automation and needs to programmatically list all versions of a registered model 'SalesForecaster' along with their current stages. Which MlflowClient call returns this information?

options.text: MlflowClient().search_model_versions("name='SalesForecaster'")
mlflow.list_registered_models()
MlflowClient().get_registered_model('SalesForecaster').versions
MlflowClient().get_latest_versions('SalesForecaster')

questions.explanation: A is correct because MlflowClient().search_model_versions() with a filter string returns all ModelVersion objects for the specified model name, each containing version number, current stage, run_id, creation timestamp, and other metadata. B (list_registered_models) returns top-level registered model objects with only the latest versions per stage, not all versions. C (get_registered_model(...).versions) is not how the response object is structured — registered model objects return latest_versions, not all versions. D (get_latest_versions) returns only the latest version in each stage, not all historical versions.
questions.reference: MlflowClient — search_model_versions() for complete version history

questions.stem: An ML engineer logs a PyTorch model and wants consumers to load it without needing PyTorch installed in their environment. They wrap the model in a custom PythonModel class that uses ONNX Runtime for inference. Which mlflow.pyfunc.log_model() argument correctly stores the ONNX model file as an accessible artifact?

options.text: mlflow.pyfunc.log_model('model', python_model=MyONNXWrapper(), artifacts={'onnx_model': '/tmp/model.onnx'})
mlflow.pyfunc.log_model('model', python_model=MyONNXWrapper(), conda_env={'channels': ['defaults']})
mlflow.onnx.log_model(onnx_model, 'model', python_model=MyONNXWrapper())
mlflow.pyfunc.log_model('model', python_model=MyONNXWrapper(), data_path='/tmp/model.onnx')

questions.explanation: A is correct because the artifacts parameter in mlflow.pyfunc.log_model() maps logical names to local file paths. At log time, MLflow copies these files into the run's artifact store. At load time, the custom PythonModel's load_context() method receives the artifacts dict with the resolved paths, allowing the ONNX model to be loaded from self.context.artifacts['onnx_model']. B only specifies the conda environment and does not copy the ONNX file. C incorrectly mixes the onnx flavor log_model signature with a python_model argument (these are separate flavors). D uses a non-existent data_path parameter.
questions.reference: MLflow pyfunc — artifacts parameter in log_model() for custom PythonModel

questions.stem: A data scientist needs to load a specific model version (version 5) from the MLflow Model Registry to reproduce a historical prediction. Which URI should they use with mlflow.pyfunc.load_model()?

options.text: models:/FraudDetector/5
models:/FraudDetector/Production/5
mlflow://FraudDetector?version=5
registry://FraudDetector/version/5

questions.explanation: A is correct because the MLflow Model Registry URI format for loading a specific version is models:/ModelName/VersionNumber. This bypasses stage references entirely and loads exactly the specified version, which is useful for reproducibility and auditing. B incorrectly adds a stage before the version number — the URI only contains model name and either a stage name or a version number, not both. C and D are invalid URI schemes not recognized by MLflow.
questions.reference: MLflow Model Registry — URI format models:/ModelName/Version

questions.stem: An ML platform team implements model governance with the requirement that every Production model must have a non-empty description and at least one reviewer tag. Using MlflowClient, which combination of calls enforces this at promotion time?

options.text: Check version.description is not None and version.tags contains 'reviewer', then call transition_model_version_stage()
Call transition_model_version_stage() with validation_callback parameter
Use mlflow.models.validate_requirements() before calling transition_model_version_stage()
Configure a registry webhook that auto-validates models before stage transition

questions.explanation: A is correct because MlflowClient provides get_model_version() to retrieve version metadata including description and tags. Governance logic should programmatically check (1) that version.description is populated and (2) that a reviewer tag exists in the model version's tags using MlflowClient().get_model_version_by_alias() or search_model_versions(), and only then call transition_model_version_stage(). This is the pattern for programmatic governance gates in MLOps pipelines. B is wrong because transition_model_version_stage() has no validation_callback parameter. C (mlflow.models.validate_requirements) validates model dependencies, not governance metadata. D (webhooks) can be configured for notifications but are not a client-side governance check before transition.
questions.reference: MlflowClient — governance gates before transition_model_version_stage()

questions.stem: A team builds an end-to-end ML pipeline using Databricks Feature Store. After model training, they call fs.log_model() instead of mlflow.sklearn.log_model(). Select TWO advantages of using fs.log_model() over mlflow.sklearn.log_model().

options.text: The logged model retains metadata about which feature tables and feature keys were used during training, enabling automatic feature lookup at inference time via score_batch()
fs.log_model() automatically optimizes the model's hyperparameters before logging
The model can be scored using FeatureStoreClient.score_batch(), which retrieves features automatically without requiring the caller to pre-join feature tables
fs.log_model() compresses the model artifact to reduce storage by 50%

questions.explanation: A is correct because FeatureStoreClient.log_model() records the feature table lineage (which tables, which keys, which features) as model metadata. This lineage is later used at inference time to automatically fetch features. C is correct because this lineage metadata enables score_batch() to take a DataFrame containing only entity primary keys and automatically join the correct features from the Feature Store, without the caller needing to know which feature tables exist or how to join them. B is wrong — fs.log_model() does not perform hyperparameter optimization. D is wrong — Feature Store model logging does not apply compression algorithms.
questions.reference: Databricks Feature Store — fs.log_model() for feature lineage and score_batch()

questions.stem: A data scientist logs a model to an MLflow run but does not register it to the Model Registry. Later, a stakeholder requests access to that model. What is the URI format to load the model directly from the run artifact?

options.text: models:/run_id/artifact_path
runs:/run_id/artifact_path
mlflow://tracking_server/run_id/artifact_path
artifacts:/run_id/artifact_path

questions.explanation: B is correct because the run-based URI format in MLflow is runs:/<run_id>/<artifact_path>, where run_id is the UUID of the training run and artifact_path is the path within that run's artifact directory (e.g., 'model' or 'my_model/sklearn'). This URI can be used directly with mlflow.pyfunc.load_model() without requiring registry registration. A uses models:/ which is the registry URI prefix, not for run artifacts. C is not a valid MLflow URI scheme. D (artifacts:/) is not a valid MLflow URI format.
questions.reference: MLflow — runs:/<run_id>/<artifact_path> URI for unregistered models

questions.stem: An MLflow model was logged with the 'tensorflow' flavor. Which load function returns the native TensorFlow SavedModel object (not the pyfunc wrapper)?

options.text: mlflow.pyfunc.load_model('runs:/abc/model')
mlflow.tensorflow.load_model('runs:/abc/model')
tf.saved_model.load('runs:/abc/model')
mlflow.load_model('runs:/abc/model', flavor='tensorflow')

questions.explanation: B is correct because mlflow.tensorflow.load_model() loads the native TensorFlow SavedModel object, giving access to TensorFlow-specific APIs like model.signatures, custom training steps, and SavedModel metadata. A (mlflow.pyfunc.load_model) wraps the model in a generic pyfunc interface and returns a PyFuncModel, hiding TensorFlow-specific functionality. C is wrong because tf.saved_model.load() expects a file system path, not an MLflow URI. D uses a non-existent flavor parameter for mlflow.load_model().
questions.reference: MLflow TensorFlow flavor — mlflow.tensorflow.load_model() for native object

questions.stem: A Unity Catalog-based MLflow registry stores a model 'main.ml_models.ChurnPredictor'. A data scientist wants to assign the alias 'champion' to version 3 and 'challenger' to version 4. Which code is correct?

options.text: MlflowClient().transition_model_version_stage('main.ml_models.ChurnPredictor', 3, 'champion')
MlflowClient().set_registered_model_alias('main.ml_models.ChurnPredictor', 'champion', 3)
MlflowClient().set_model_version_tag('main.ml_models.ChurnPredictor', 3, 'alias', 'champion')
mlflow.register_model('models:/main.ml_models.ChurnPredictor/3', alias='champion')

questions.explanation: B is correct because in the Unity Catalog registry, model aliases (replacing stages) are managed via MlflowClient().set_registered_model_alias(model_name, alias, version). This assigns a named alias to a specific version, enabling the URI models:/main.ml_models.ChurnPredictor@champion for loading. A uses transition_model_version_stage() which is a classic registry concept not available in Unity Catalog — UC uses aliases instead of stages. C (set_model_version_tag) sets a tag key-value pair, which is not the alias system. D misuses register_model() with a non-existent alias parameter.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog Registry — set_registered_model_alias() for model aliases

questions.stem: A Feature Store table 'catalog.features.user_profile' exists with primary key 'user_id'. A data scientist wants to retrieve features for users in a labels DataFrame for model training. Which FeatureLookup configuration correctly specifies this?

options.text: FeatureLookup(table_name='catalog.features.user_profile', feature_names=['age', 'tenure'], lookup_key='user_id')
FeatureLookup(table='catalog.features.user_profile', key='user_id', features=['age', 'tenure'])
FeatureLookup(feature_table='user_profile', join_key='user_id')
FeatureJoin(table_name='catalog.features.user_profile', on='user_id', select=['age', 'tenure'])

questions.explanation: A is correct because the Databricks Feature Store FeatureLookup constructor takes table_name (the full three-part catalog.schema.table name), feature_names (the list of feature columns to retrieve from that table), and lookup_key (the column in the labels DataFrame that maps to the feature table's primary key). B uses incorrect parameter names (table, key, features instead of table_name, lookup_key, feature_names). C uses incorrect parameter names and a non-three-part table name. D (FeatureJoin) is not a valid Databricks Feature Store class.
questions.reference: Databricks Feature Store — FeatureLookup constructor parameters

questions.stem: An ML team deploys a model to Databricks Model Serving. During a low-traffic period, they notice the endpoint is still incurring compute costs. What configuration change can reduce costs during idle periods while maintaining availability for incoming requests?

options.text: Set the minimum provisioned throughput to 0 to enable scale-to-zero
Reduce the model version number to a smaller artifact size
Switch from a GPU to a CPU instance type
Set the endpoint status to 'paused' between business hours

questions.explanation: A is correct because Databricks Model Serving supports scale-to-zero when minimum provisioned throughput is set to 0 (or for CPU endpoints, when the minimum number of instances is set to 0). The endpoint automatically scales down to zero replicas during idle periods and scales back up when requests arrive (incurring a cold start latency). This is the standard cost optimization for low-traffic endpoints. B (model version number) is unrelated to compute cost. C (GPU to CPU) reduces per-unit cost but does not eliminate idle cost. D — pausing endpoints is possible but requires management automation and creates full downtime, whereas scale-to-zero maintains availability.
questions.reference: Databricks Model Serving — scale-to-zero for cost optimization

questions.stem: An ML engineer deploys batch inference using mlflow.pyfunc.spark_udf() and needs predictions to be stored as FloatType in the output DataFrame. Which parameter controls the output data type of the Spark UDF?

options.text: output_dtype=FloatType() in mlflow.pyfunc.spark_udf()
result_type='float' in mlflow.pyfunc.spark_udf()
Cast the output column after applying the UDF: df.withColumn('pred', col('pred').cast('float'))
The output type is always inferred from the model signature and cannot be overridden

questions.explanation: B is correct because mlflow.pyfunc.spark_udf() accepts a result_type parameter that specifies the Spark return type of the UDF. It accepts either a PySpark DataType object or a string shorthand like 'float', 'double', 'int', or 'ArrayType(FloatType())' for array outputs. A uses a non-existent output_dtype parameter name. C would work as a workaround but is not the direct UDF parameter — the question asks about the parameter that controls the type. D is wrong because result_type can always be specified explicitly.
questions.reference: mlflow.pyfunc.spark_udf() — result_type parameter

questions.stem: A data science team is deciding between deploying a model as a Databricks Model Serving endpoint versus a batch Spark UDF job. Select TWO scenarios where the REST endpoint approach is clearly preferred.

options.text: A mobile app needs real-time product recommendations with under 200ms latency on each user tap
A nightly ETL job needs to score 50 million records and write results to a Delta table by 6am
An online checkout flow needs fraud probability scores synchronously before each transaction is approved
A data team wants to recompute feature importance scores monthly for a model governance report

questions.explanation: A is correct because real-time product recommendations with sub-200ms latency require a low-latency REST endpoint that can respond synchronously to each user interaction. Batch Spark jobs have startup overhead incompatible with this SLA. C is correct because fraud detection must return a score synchronously before each transaction is approved — a REST endpoint is the only viable architecture for this synchronous, low-latency, per-request use case. B (nightly ETL scoring 50M records) is a classic batch workload best served by a Spark UDF for throughput efficiency. D (monthly feature importance) is clearly batch/offline work with no real-time requirement.
questions.reference: Model Deployment — real-time serving endpoint use cases: latency and synchronous requirements

questions.stem: A data science team logs a model with a custom conda environment that requires Python 3.9 and scikit-learn 1.2.0. When using mlflow.pyfunc.spark_udf() with env_manager='conda', what happens on a cluster worker that currently runs Python 3.10?

options.text: The UDF fails immediately because the Python version mismatch prevents conda environment creation
Conda creates a new isolated virtual environment on the worker with Python 3.9 and scikit-learn 1.2.0, and the UDF runs within that environment
MLflow automatically upgrades the cluster to Python 3.9 for all processes
The UDF runs using Python 3.10 but installs scikit-learn 1.2.0 into the existing environment

questions.explanation: B is correct because env_manager='conda' instructs MLflow to create a new isolated conda environment on each worker according to the model's conda.yaml, including the specified Python version (3.9) and all package dependencies. This environment is completely separate from the cluster's base environment. A is wrong — conda can create new environments with different Python versions than the base; this is a primary use case of conda. C is wrong — MLflow does not modify the cluster's base Python version. D is wrong — with env_manager='conda', MLflow does not install into the existing environment.
questions.reference: mlflow.pyfunc.spark_udf() — conda environment isolation with env_manager='conda'

questions.stem: An ML team is running A/B testing with 70%/30% traffic split between model version 5 (champion) and version 6 (challenger) on a Databricks Model Serving endpoint. After 1 week, they discover version 6 has higher latency. Before fully rolling back to version 5, what is the safest intermediate step?

options.text: Delete version 6 from the registry to immediately stop all traffic to it
Update the traffic split to 95%/5% (version 5 / version 6) to minimize user impact while retaining A/B capability for further investigation
Deploy a third model version 7 to take version 6's traffic allocation
Disable the serving endpoint entirely until the investigation completes

questions.explanation: B is correct because reducing the challenger's traffic share (e.g., from 30% to 5%) minimizes user impact from the latency issue while maintaining observability — the team can continue gathering latency samples at low volume to diagnose the root cause before deciding to fully roll back or fix. A (deleting version 6) is destructive and eliminates the ability to investigate the model or its artifacts. C (adding version 7) introduces unnecessary complexity before understanding the version 6 issue. D (disabling the endpoint) causes complete service outage, which is more disruptive than the latency issue itself.
questions.reference: Databricks Model Serving — incremental traffic reduction for safe rollback

questions.stem:
A data scientist uses the following code to apply a model to a Spark DataFrame:

```python
predict_udf = mlflow.pyfunc.spark_udf(spark, model_uri, result_type='double')
df_out = input_df.withColumn('score', predict_udf(struct(*input_df.columns)))
```

Why does this code use struct(*input_df.columns) instead of listing individual columns?

options.text: struct() is required by all Spark UDFs and has no special meaning in this context
Passing a struct column allows the pyfunc UDF to receive a pandas DataFrame with named columns inside the predict() method, matching the model's expected input format
struct() reduces memory usage by compressing column values before passing them to the UDF
The UDF must receive a single argument; struct() packs all columns into one argument

questions.explanation: B is correct because MLflow pyfunc UDFs are designed to receive either individual column arguments (passed as separate args) or a struct column. When using struct(), the UDF receives the data as a pandas DataFrame with column names preserved, which matches the format expected by model.predict() (which typically expects a named DataFrame or dict). This is the recommended pattern when the model has a signature with named input columns. A is wrong — struct() is not required by all UDFs; it's specific to the pyfunc pattern. C is wrong — struct() does not compress data. D is partially correct (single argument), but misses the key reason: named column access inside predict().
questions.reference: mlflow.pyfunc.spark_udf() — struct() pattern for named column input

questions.stem: An ML team wants to serve a large language model on Databricks Model Serving with custom preprocessing and the ability to stream tokens in the response. Which serving configuration or feature enables token streaming?

options.text: Setting result_type='streaming' in the endpoint configuration
Using the External Models endpoint type configured with a streaming-capable model provider
Enabling the Server-Sent Events (SSE) streaming option on the Foundation Model API endpoint or custom model with streaming predict
Deploying the model as a batch inference job with micro-batches of single tokens

questions.explanation: C is correct because Databricks Model Serving supports streaming responses via Server-Sent Events (SSE) for Foundation Model API endpoints and for custom models that implement a streaming predict() method. The client receives tokens as they are generated rather than waiting for the complete response, enabling progressive UX patterns. A (result_type='streaming') is not a valid endpoint configuration option. B (External Models) can also support streaming but only for specific providers, and the more general answer is C. D (micro-batch single tokens) is not how streaming inference works and would have extreme overhead per token.
questions.reference: Databricks Model Serving — streaming responses with SSE for Foundation Models

questions.stem: A data scientist uses mlflow.pyfunc.spark_udf() for batch inference on a dataset with 500 million rows partitioned across 200 Spark partitions. They observe that 10 partitions are processing much slower than others. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The MLflow model is not thread-safe and serializes predictions across all 200 partitions
Data skew — those 10 partitions contain significantly more rows than the average partition
The conda environment creation takes longer on some workers than others
The model signature validation is more expensive for certain row values

questions.explanation: B is correct because data skew is the most common cause of stragglers in distributed Spark batch inference. If the data is not uniformly distributed across partitions (e.g., due to an unbalanced key distribution before repartitioning), some partitions will have many more rows, causing those tasks to run much longer than others. The fix is to repartition the input DataFrame more evenly before applying the UDF. A is wrong — MLflow pyfunc UDFs run in separate Python worker processes per executor task, so thread safety is not the issue. C is wrong — conda environment creation happens once per worker, not per partition. D is wrong — signature validation is O(1) per row for type checking, not variable.
questions.reference: Spark batch inference — data skew causing partition stragglers

questions.stem: A machine learning team wants to detect when the statistical relationship between features and the target label has changed in production, even though the input feature distributions remain stable. Which type of drift does this describe?

options.text: Data drift (covariate shift)
Concept drift
Prior probability shift
Feature correlation drift

questions.explanation: B is correct because concept drift refers specifically to changes in the conditional distribution P(Y|X) — the relationship between features and labels — while the input distribution P(X) remains stable. This is particularly dangerous because standard feature-level drift detectors would not trigger an alert, yet the model's mapping has become stale. A (data drift/covariate shift) is when P(X) changes but P(Y|X) is assumed stable. C (prior probability shift) is a change in P(Y) — the label distribution — independent of features. D (feature correlation drift) is not a standard ML monitoring term.
questions.reference: Monitoring — concept drift: P(Y|X) changes while P(X) remains stable

questions.stem: An ML team is monitoring a regression model and observes that the mean of the predicted scores has shifted from 0.72 at training time to 0.85 in production over the past month, but no ground truth labels are available yet. What is the most actionable monitoring response?

options.text: Immediately retrain the model since the shift confirms performance degradation
Investigate whether input feature distributions have shifted (data drift) to explain the prediction shift, and alert stakeholders for review
Accept the shift as normal model behavior and adjust the decision threshold accordingly
Log a model version tag indicating the shift and continue without investigation

questions.explanation: B is correct because a shift in prediction distribution without ground truth labels is a warning signal, not conclusive proof of degradation. The correct response is to (1) run feature drift analysis to check if input distributions have changed (which could explain the prediction shift), (2) alert stakeholders, and (3) expedite ground truth label collection. A is premature — without labels, we cannot confirm degradation vs. legitimate distribution change in the target population. C is premature adjustment without understanding the root cause. D (logging a tag only) is insufficient — this warrants active investigation.
questions.reference: Monitoring — prediction drift without ground truth: investigate upstream feature drift

questions.stem: A Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring job is configured for a classification model's inference table. Select TWO metrics that Lakehouse Monitoring can compute automatically from an inference table containing predictions, labels, and timestamps.

options.text: Accuracy and F1 score per time window, comparing predictions to ground truth labels
Jensen-Shannon divergence of the prediction score distribution versus the training baseline
The business revenue impact of incorrect predictions
Count and percentage of null values in each input feature column over time

questions.explanation: A is correct because Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring's InferenceLog monitor computes model quality metrics (accuracy, F1, precision, recall for classification; RMSE, MAE for regression) per time window by comparing the prediction column against the label column. B is correct because Lakehouse Monitoring also computes distribution drift metrics (including Jensen-Shannon divergence and other statistical distance measures) for prediction score distributions and input features over configurable time windows. C is wrong because business revenue impact requires domain-specific business logic that Lakehouse Monitoring cannot compute automatically. D is partially correct (null counts are tracked in profiling) but D as stated is about data quality, which is included in profile monitors — however, between the two correct options, A and B are more specifically what Lakehouse Monitoring automates for ML inference tables.
questions.reference: Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring — InferenceLog: model quality metrics and drift statistics

questions.stem: An ML platform team wants to build an automated retraining pipeline that triggers when drift is detected. They use Databricks Workflows to orchestrate the pipeline. Which architectural pattern correctly implements the trigger mechanism?

options.text: Configure the monitoring job to write a drift alert to a Delta table; use a Databricks Workflow with a File Arrival trigger on that table to launch the retraining job
Manually check the Lakehouse Monitoring dashboard daily and click 'Retrain' when drift is observed
Set a cron schedule to retrain every 24 hours regardless of drift detection
Use a Databricks Workflow with a Continuous trigger and add drift threshold checking as the first task; downstream tasks conditionally execute retraining if drift exceeds the threshold

questions.explanation: D is correct because a Databricks Workflow with a Continuous trigger (or a scheduled trigger running frequently) where the first task checks drift metrics from the monitoring output tables and conditionally triggers downstream retraining tasks represents a robust automated pipeline. This is the recommended MLOps pattern: the workflow reads from the Lakehouse Monitoring drift metrics table, evaluates PSI/KS thresholds, and uses conditional task execution (if/else logic in the workflow DAG or a task that raises an exception to skip downstream tasks) to retrain only when needed. A (File Arrival trigger on a Delta table) works but Databricks Workflows' File Arrival trigger is for DBFS/object storage paths, not Delta tables — the pattern is valid in concept but the trigger mechanism is more naturally implemented via D. B is manual, not automated. C (time-based retraining) ignores drift signals and retrains unnecessarily, wasting resources.
questions.reference: MLOps — automated retraining pipeline with drift-conditional Databricks Workflows

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to distinguish between metadata that describes the run (e.g., team name, project phase) and hyperparameters that define the model configuration. Which MLflow functions best serve these two purposes respectively?

options.text: mlflow.log_metric() for metadata; mlflow.log_param() for hyperparameters
mlflow.set_tag() for metadata; mlflow.log_param() for hyperparameters
mlflow.log_param() for both — use different key prefixes to distinguish them
mlflow.set_tag() for hyperparameters; mlflow.log_metric() for metadata

questions.explanation: B is correct because mlflow.set_tag() is designed for descriptive, organizational metadata — team name, experiment phase, Git branch, dataset version — that labels and annotates a run without being part of its configuration. mlflow.log_param() is designed for hyperparameters and configuration values that define the training setup (learning rate, number of layers, regularization strength). Tags are free-form strings visible in the Tags column of the MLflow UI; params appear in the Parameters section. A is wrong because log_metric() is for numeric scalar metrics, not metadata. C conflates the two concepts in a single namespace. D reverses the roles of set_tag and log_metric.
questions.reference: MLflow — mlflow.set_tag() for metadata vs mlflow.log_param() for hyperparameters

questions.stem: An ML engineer uses MlflowClient().get_run('abc123') to programmatically inspect a completed run. They want to read the value of parameter 'max_depth'. Which expression is correct?

options.text: run.params['max_depth']
run.data.params['max_depth']
run.info.params['max_depth']
run.data['params']['max_depth']

questions.explanation: B is correct because MlflowClient().get_run() returns an mlflow.entities.Run object. Parameters are stored in run.data — specifically run.data.params, which is a dict mapping parameter names to their string values. A is wrong because there is no direct run.params attribute; the RunData object is accessed via run.data. C is wrong because run.info contains run metadata (run_id, experiment_id, lifecycle_stage, start_time) — not logged values. D is wrong because run.data is a RunData object, not a plain dict with string keys.
questions.reference: MlflowClient — get_run() and accessing run.data.params

questions.stem: A data scientist uses hp.loguniform('weight_decay', math.log(1e-6), math.log(1e-2)) in HyperOpt. What type of values does this expression sample, and why is this appropriate for a weight decay hyperparameter?

options.text: Values sampled uniformly from the range [log(1e-6), log(1e-2)], i.e., negative numbers between -13.8 and -4.6
Values sampled such that their logarithm is uniformly distributed — i.e., multiplicatively uniform between 1e-6 and 1e-2 — giving equal search coverage per order of magnitude
Integer values: -6, -5, -4, -3, -2, representing powers of 10
Values sampled from a log-normal distribution centered at 1e-4

questions.explanation: B is correct because hp.loguniform(label, low, high) returns exp(uniform(low, high)), producing values that are multiplicatively uniform — equal probability mass is assigned to each factor-of-10 range (1e-6 to 1e-5, 1e-5 to 1e-4, etc.). For weight decay, which spans multiple orders of magnitude and where sensitivity is relative rather than absolute, log-uniform sampling ensures the small end of the range (1e-6 to 1e-5) gets as much exploration as the large end (1e-3 to 1e-2). A is wrong — the sampled values are exponentiated, not the log-space bounds. C is wrong — hp.loguniform is continuous, not discrete. D describes a log-normal distribution, which is different from log-uniform.
questions.reference: HyperOpt — hp.loguniform for multiplicatively uniform sampling of weight decay

questions.stem: A data scientist runs mlflow.evaluate() on a regression model, passing data=eval_df, targets='label', model_type='regressor'. After evaluation, they want to access the computed RMSE programmatically. Which expression retrieves it?

options.text: result.metrics['root_mean_squared_error']
mlflow.get_run(result.run_id).data.metrics['rmse']
result['metrics']['rmse']
result.artifacts['evaluation_metrics']['rmse']

questions.explanation: A is correct because mlflow.evaluate() returns an mlflow.models.EvaluationResult object. The metrics dict on this object contains the computed metrics with their standard names as keys. For regression models, the RMSE is stored under the key 'root_mean_squared_error' (the full name, not the abbreviation 'rmse'). These metrics are also simultaneously logged to the active MLflow run. B is wrong — while the metrics are logged to the run, the key would be 'root_mean_squared_error' not 'rmse'; also the run_id is accessed via result.run_id but this is an indirect path. C is wrong because EvaluationResult is not subscriptable with string keys. D is wrong because metrics are in result.metrics, not result.artifacts.
questions.reference: MLflow — mlflow.evaluate() EvaluationResult.metrics['root_mean_squared_error']

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to log a dataset reference to an MLflow run using the dataset logging API. They want to record both the source path and the dataset's MD5 hash for data integrity verification. Which mlflow.data function creates a Dataset object from a pandas DataFrame with these properties?

options.text: mlflow.data.from_pandas(df, source='dbfs:/data/train.parquet', name='train_data')
mlflow.data.dataset(df, path='dbfs:/data/train.parquet', hash=True)
mlflow.log_artifact('dbfs:/data/train.parquet', artifact_path='datasets')
mlflow.data.PandasDataset(df, source='dbfs:/data/train.parquet')

questions.explanation: A is correct because mlflow.data.from_pandas() is the standard factory function for creating an MLflow Dataset object from a pandas DataFrame. It accepts a source parameter (the URI where the data originated), an optional name for the dataset, and optionally a targets column name. The from_pandas() function automatically computes a hash of the DataFrame's contents for data integrity. B uses a non-existent mlflow.data.dataset() function. C logs the raw file as an artifact but does not create a structured Dataset object with schema and hash metadata. D instantiates PandasDataset directly — while PandasDataset is the underlying class, the recommended API is from_pandas().
questions.reference: MLflow — mlflow.data.from_pandas() for creating Dataset objects with source and hash

questions.stem: A data scientist calls mlflow.evaluate() on a binary classifier, passing evaluators=['default']. Select TWO artifacts that are automatically generated and logged by the default evaluator for a binary classifier.

options.text: A confusion matrix visualization logged as a PNG artifact
An ROC curve plot logged as an artifact
SHAP beeswarm plots for each feature
A hyperparameter importance plot showing which params most influenced accuracy

questions.explanation: A is correct because the default evaluator for classifiers automatically generates and logs a confusion matrix as a visual artifact alongside the confusion matrix values as a metric. B is correct because the ROC curve (plot of TPR vs. FPR at various thresholds) is automatically generated and logged as a PNG artifact by the default classifier evaluator. C is wrong — SHAP visualizations require the 'shap' evaluator to be explicitly included; they are not part of the 'default' evaluator. D is wrong — hyperparameter importance plots are not generated by mlflow.evaluate(); they would require custom analysis with tools like Optuna's importance visualization.
questions.reference: MLflow — mlflow.evaluate() default evaluator artifacts: confusion matrix and ROC curve

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to retrieve the default artifact storage URI for an experiment named 'ChurnExperiment'. Given exp = MlflowClient().get_experiment_by_name('ChurnExperiment'), which attribute holds the artifact location?

options.text: exp.artifact_root
exp.artifact_location
exp.default_artifact_uri
exp.data.artifact_location

questions.explanation: B is correct because the Experiment object returned by get_experiment_by_name() stores the artifact root directory URI in the artifact_location attribute. This is the base URI where all run artifacts for this experiment are stored by default (e.g., 'dbfs:/databricks/mlflow-tracking/<experiment_id>'). A (artifact_root) is not an attribute on the Experiment object. C (default_artifact_uri) is not a standard Experiment attribute. D is wrong because Experiment objects are not structured with a nested .data intermediate like Run objects.
questions.reference: MlflowClient — Experiment.artifact_location attribute

questions.stem: An ML engineer trains a parent ensemble model with three child base learner runs using nested=True. They want to log the ensemble's combined OOF (out-of-fold) predictions as an artifact on the parent run while child runs are still open. Which pattern achieves this correctly?

options.text: Log the artifact inside any child run context — it will automatically propagate to the parent
Store the parent run_id before opening child runs; after closing all child contexts, reopen the parent with mlflow.start_run(run_id=parent_run_id) and log the artifact
Use mlflow.log_artifact() with a parent_run_id parameter
Artifacts cannot be added to a parent run that has child runs

questions.explanation: B is correct because MLflow does not propagate artifacts from child to parent runs automatically. The correct pattern is: (1) capture the parent run_id from mlflow.active_run().info.run_id before entering child run contexts, (2) after all child contexts are closed, reopen the parent run using mlflow.start_run(run_id=parent_run_id) as a context manager, and (3) log artifacts to the parent. This is the standard technique for logging ensemble-level artifacts. A is wrong — artifacts logged in child contexts stay on the child runs. C is wrong — log_artifact() has no parent_run_id parameter. D is false — parent runs can receive artifacts at any time they are active.
questions.reference: MLflow nested runs — logging artifacts to parent run after child runs complete

questions.stem: A data scientist logs 50 parameters to an MLflow run at once using mlflow.log_params(params_dict). Later they realize one parameter name was misspelled. What is the correct way to fix the misspelled parameter in the existing run?

options.text: Call mlflow.log_param('correct_name', value) in the same run — MLflow will add it; the misspelled key persists but can be ignored
Delete the run and re-create it with the corrected parameter name
Call mlflow.update_param('misspelled_name', 'correct_name') to rename the parameter
MLflow parameters are immutable once logged — the misspelled key cannot be deleted or renamed, only supplemented with a correctly named key

questions.explanation: D is correct because MLflow parameters are immutable — once a key-value pair is logged, it cannot be updated or deleted through the MLflow API. The practical resolution is to log the corrected parameter name with the correct value (mlflow.log_param('correct_name', value)) while accepting that the misspelled key remains. For production pipelines, this underscores the importance of validating parameter names before logging. A is partially correct in describing the outcome but incompletely describes the situation — the misspelled key is not ignorable in automated pipelines that filter by param name. B (deleting and re-creating) loses all other logged data unless it is all re-logged. C uses a non-existent mlflow.update_param() function.
questions.reference: MLflow — parameter immutability: logged params cannot be updated or deleted

questions.stem: A data scientist registers a model from an active MLflow run using mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, 'model', registered_model_name='InventoryModel'). What version number is assigned to this model in the registry?

options.text: Version 0, since Python uses 0-based indexing
Version 1 if this is the first version registered under 'InventoryModel'; otherwise the next sequential integer
The same as the MLflow run ID
A random UUID assigned at registration time

questions.explanation: B is correct because MLflow Model Registry assigns sequential integer version numbers starting from 1. The first model registered under a given name receives version 1, the second version 2, and so on. Version numbers are monotonically increasing and cannot be reset or reused — even if earlier versions are deleted. A is wrong — MLflow uses 1-based version numbering. C is wrong — run IDs are UUIDs, not integers; they are not used as version numbers. D is wrong — version numbers are sequential integers, not random UUIDs.
questions.reference: MLflow Model Registry — sequential integer versioning starting from 1

questions.stem: An ML team uses Unity Catalog and wants to verify that model 'prod.ml.RiskScorer' has an alias 'champion' pointing to version 8, before deploying. Which call retrieves the version number that 'champion' currently points to?

options.text: MlflowClient().get_registered_model_alias('prod.ml.RiskScorer', 'champion').version
MlflowClient().get_model_version_by_alias('prod.ml.RiskScorer', 'champion').version
mlflow.pyfunc.load_model('models:/prod.ml.RiskScorer@champion').version
MlflowClient().search_model_versions("name='prod.ml.RiskScorer' and alias='champion'").version

questions.explanation: B is correct because MlflowClient().get_model_version_by_alias(name, alias) returns a ModelVersion object for the version currently associated with that alias. The version number is then accessible via .version attribute. This is the dedicated API for alias-to-version resolution in the Unity Catalog registry. A uses get_registered_model_alias() which is not a valid MlflowClient method. C (load_model) loads the full model into memory just to read a version number, which is unnecessarily expensive and the loaded pyfunc object has no .version attribute. D uses search_model_versions() with an invalid alias filter parameter (filter_string does not support alias= conditions).
questions.reference: MlflowClient — get_model_version_by_alias() for Unity Catalog alias resolution

questions.stem: A data scientist logs a model with mlflow.pyfunc.log_model() and passes a pip_requirements list. A colleague reviews the logged model artifacts and finds both a requirements.txt and a conda.yaml. How do these two files relate to each other?

options.text: They are identical files with different names for compatibility with different serving environments
requirements.txt contains only pip packages; conda.yaml contains the same pip packages plus conda channels and python version, providing the complete conda environment specification
conda.yaml is automatically generated and requirements.txt must be manually provided — the two are independent
requirements.txt is used for container-based serving; conda.yaml is used for Spark UDF serving with env_manager='conda'

questions.explanation: B is correct because when MLflow logs a model, it generates both a requirements.txt (pip-only format listing package==version entries) and a conda.yaml (full conda environment format with channels, pip sections, and Python version). The conda.yaml is the superset — it includes the Python version specification, conda channel configuration, and the pip packages. requirements.txt is a subset used by lightweight serving environments that do not have conda. Both are automatically generated from the model's detected dependencies plus any extra_pip_requirements. A is wrong — they have different formats and scopes. C is wrong — both files are auto-generated by MLflow. D is wrong — the choice of which file is used depends on env_manager, but both are present and auto-generated.
questions.reference: MLflow model artifacts — requirements.txt vs conda.yaml: pip-only vs full conda spec

questions.stem: An ML team is designing a webhook-based governance workflow for their MLflow Model Registry. Select TWO valid use cases for registry webhooks.

options.text: Triggering an automated model validation pipeline (unit tests, bias checks) whenever a new model version is registered via the MODEL_VERSION_CREATED event
Automatically retraining the model whenever the serving endpoint receives more than 1,000 requests per minute
Notifying the risk management team via a Slack webhook whenever a model version transitions to Production via the MODEL_VERSION_TRANSITIONED_STAGE event
Preventing unauthorized users from loading models via models:/ URI by intercepting load_model calls

questions.explanation: A is correct because MODEL_VERSION_CREATED webhooks are commonly used to trigger automated validation pipelines — the webhook fires when a new version is registered, calling an HTTP endpoint that launches a Databricks Job for testing and validation before the version is promoted. C is correct because MODEL_VERSION_TRANSITIONED_STAGE webhooks fire after stage changes, enabling notifications to downstream teams (e.g., via Slack webhook or PagerDuty) about production deployments. B is wrong — webhook events are registry lifecycle events, not serving endpoint metrics events; load-based triggers are implemented via endpoint monitoring, not registry webhooks. D is wrong — registry webhooks are notification/trigger mechanisms, not access control interceptors; authorization is handled by Unity Catalog permissions.
questions.reference: MLflow Registry Webhooks — MODEL_VERSION_CREATED and MODEL_VERSION_TRANSITIONED_STAGE use cases

questions.stem: A data scientist calls mlflow.artifacts.download_artifacts(artifact_uri='runs:/abc123/model', dst_path='/tmp/inspect'). After the download, which file in /tmp/inspect/ is the primary manifest that describes the model's flavors, signature, and metadata?

options.text: model.pkl
conda.yaml
MLmodel
requirements.txt

questions.explanation: C is correct because the MLmodel file (no extension) is the primary manifest in every MLflow model artifact directory. It is a YAML file that describes the model's flavors (e.g., python_function, sklearn), model signature (input/output schema), creation time, run_id, artifact_path, and any loader modules. MLflow's load_model functions parse the MLmodel file first to determine how to deserialize the model. A (model.pkl) is the serialized sklearn estimator for sklearn-flavor models — it is framework-specific, not the manifest. B (conda.yaml) describes the environment dependencies but not the model metadata. D (requirements.txt) lists pip packages, not model metadata.
questions.reference: MLflow model artifacts — MLmodel manifest file structure

questions.stem: A Unity Catalog model 'staging.ml.EmailClassifier' needs to be moved to the production catalog as 'prod.ml.EmailClassifier'. The team wants the same model weights without retraining. What is the correct approach?

options.text: Use MlflowClient().copy_model_version('staging.ml.EmailClassifier', 1, 'prod.ml.EmailClassifier') to copy within Unity Catalog
Load the model from 'models:/staging.ml.EmailClassifier/1' and re-log it to a new run, then register as 'prod.ml.EmailClassifier'
Rename the registered model using MlflowClient().rename_registered_model('staging.ml.EmailClassifier', 'prod.ml.EmailClassifier')
Use the Databricks Unity Catalog data sharing feature to share the model across catalogs

questions.explanation: A is correct because MlflowClient().copy_model_version() is the dedicated method for copying a model version from one registered model to another (potentially in a different catalog or schema) without retraining. The copy creates a new version in the destination registered model that references the same artifact URI. B (load and re-log) would work but creates a new run artifact and loses the original lineage. C (rename) changes the model name in place but cannot move it to a different catalog — renaming is constrained to the same registry namespace. D is wrong because data sharing is for Delta tables, not MLflow model registry objects.
questions.reference: MlflowClient — copy_model_version() for cross-catalog model promotion

questions.stem: An ML engineer is building a Feature Store pipeline with two feature tables: 'catalog.features.user_demographics' (primary key: user_id) and 'catalog.features.product_stats' (primary key: product_id). The label DataFrame has columns ['user_id', 'product_id', 'purchased']. How many FeatureLookup objects are needed to retrieve features from both tables?

options.text: One FeatureLookup with a compound primary key of ['user_id', 'product_id']
Two FeatureLookup objects — one for each feature table with its respective lookup key
One FeatureLookup pointing to a combined view that joins both feature tables
Zero FeatureLookup objects — feature tables with different primary keys cannot be combined in one training set

questions.explanation: B is correct because create_training_set() accepts a list of FeatureLookup objects, one per feature table (or feature subset). Each FeatureLookup independently specifies its own table_name and lookup_key. In this case: FeatureLookup(table_name='catalog.features.user_demographics', lookup_key='user_id', feature_names=[...]) and FeatureLookup(table_name='catalog.features.product_stats', lookup_key='product_id', feature_names=[...]). The Feature Store joins both tables to the label DataFrame in a single create_training_set() call. A is wrong because a compound key only works if a single table uses both columns as a joint primary key. C requires pre-creating a view, losing Feature Store lineage tracking. D is false — Feature Store explicitly supports multiple lookup tables with different primary keys.
questions.reference: Databricks Feature Store — multiple FeatureLookup objects for different entity types

questions.stem: A data scientist calls MlflowClient().get_run('abc123') and accesses run.data.tags. They see a tag with key 'mlflow.source.type' with value 'NOTEBOOK'. What kind of tag is this?

options.text: A user-defined tag set with mlflow.set_tag('mlflow.source.type', 'NOTEBOOK')
A system tag automatically set by MLflow/Databricks to indicate the run was started from a notebook
A tag required by mlflow.autolog() to track the source of autologged parameters
A tag that prevents the run from being deleted

questions.explanation: B is correct because MLflow and Databricks automatically set system tags (prefixed with 'mlflow.') on runs to capture provenance metadata: mlflow.source.type (NOTEBOOK, JOB, PROJECT, LOCAL), mlflow.source.name (the notebook path or script name), mlflow.user (the user who started the run), mlflow.databricks.notebookPath, etc. These system tags are set automatically and inform users about the run's origin without any explicit mlflow.set_tag() calls. A is wrong — user-defined tags would not use the reserved 'mlflow.' prefix. C is wrong — autolog does not set source.type; it is set by the Databricks MLflow tracking server. D is wrong — system tags have no lifecycle management function.
questions.reference: MLflow — system tags vs user tags: mlflow.source.type automatically set by runtime

questions.stem: A model has been registered in the classic MLflow Model Registry. The team wants to programmatically check whether archiving version 2 would leave any stage without a model version. Specifically, they want to confirm there is still a 'Production' version after archiving. Which sequence of calls achieves this check?

options.text: client.get_latest_versions('MyModel', stages=['Production']) — if it returns a non-empty list with a version other than 2, Production has another version
client.search_model_versions("name='MyModel'") — filter client-side for stage='Production' and version != 2
client.get_registered_model('MyModel').production_versions — check if length > 1
client.transition_model_version_stage('MyModel', 2, 'Archived', archive_existing_versions=True)

questions.explanation: A is correct because MlflowClient().get_latest_versions('MyModel', stages=['Production']) returns the latest version(s) currently in the Production stage. If the returned list contains a version other than version 2 (or version 2 is not the only Production version), then Production will still have a live version after archiving version 2. B also works but is less efficient (client-side filtering of all versions). C is wrong because get_registered_model() returns a RegisteredModel object that has latest_versions (not production_versions) — a synthetic attribute that may not list all concurrent production versions. D is an action, not a check — and archive_existing_versions=True would archive all current Production versions before promoting, which is the wrong sequence for a pre-check.
questions.reference: MlflowClient — get_latest_versions() for checking stage occupancy before archiving

questions.stem: A data scientist makes a REST API call to a Databricks Model Serving endpoint and receives a JSON response with a field named 'predictions'. Which payload format produces this response structure?

options.text: Request payload: {'dataframe_split': {'columns': ['f1', 'f2'], 'data': [[1.0, 2.0]]}}
Request payload: {'dataframe_records': [{'f1': 1.0, 'f2': 2.0}]}
Request payload: {'inputs': [[1.0, 2.0]]}
All three payload formats produce a 'predictions' field in the response

questions.explanation: D is correct because Databricks Model Serving endpoints (MLflow-backed) return a JSON response with a 'predictions' key regardless of the input payload format used. The response format is standardized: {'predictions': [<value>, ...]} for scalar outputs, or {'predictions': [{<col>: <val>, ...}]} for DataFrame outputs. The input format (dataframe_split, dataframe_records, or inputs) controls how the request is parsed, but the output format is always the 'predictions' wrapper. A, B, and C all describe valid input formats that each produce the same 'predictions' response structure.
questions.reference: Databricks Model Serving — REST response format: 'predictions' field for all input formats

questions.stem: An ML team runs batch inference with mlflow.pyfunc.spark_udf() on a DataFrame with 1 billion rows. The job takes 6 hours. A colleague suggests repartitioning the DataFrame to 2,000 partitions before applying the UDF (currently 200 partitions). Under what condition would this improve performance?

options.text: Only if the cluster has more than 2,000 CPU cores available to process all partitions in parallel
If the cluster has sufficient parallelism and each current partition is large enough that breaking it into 10 smaller partitions reduces per-task time and improves resource utilization
Increasing partitions always improves performance by reducing task size
Repartitioning to 2,000 partitions would not help — the bottleneck is model inference speed, not partition count

questions.explanation: B is correct because repartitioning improves performance when (1) the cluster has sufficient cores to run many tasks in parallel and (2) current partitions are large enough that the per-task execution time is long, causing cluster under-utilization during the last few stragglers. Breaking 200 large partitions into 2,000 smaller ones allows better load balancing and higher parallelism if the cluster has the capacity. A is wrong — 2,000 cores are not required; you just need more than 200 concurrent task slots. C is wrong — excessive partitioning creates scheduler overhead and empty partitions that hurt performance. D is wrong — partition count directly affects parallelism, which is orthogonal to per-row inference speed.
questions.reference: Spark batch inference — repartitioning for improved parallelism and load balancing

questions.stem: An ML engineer deploys a pyfunc model to Databricks Model Serving. The model's predict() method calls an external feature store (hosted on Redis) to retrieve real-time features for each request. During load testing, the endpoint shows high p99 latency spikes. What is the most likely cause and fix?

options.text: The Redis client is being re-initialized on every request; fix by initializing the Redis connection in load_context() and reusing it in predict()
Redis is not supported as an external dependency in Databricks Model Serving endpoints
The predict() method should use a thread pool to make Redis calls asynchronously
Switch to DynamoDB — Redis is too slow for Model Serving use cases

questions.explanation: A is correct because initializing a Redis client (including TCP handshake, authentication, and connection pool setup) inside predict() would incur that overhead on every single request, causing consistent latency. The fix is to initialize the Redis client in load_context() (which runs once when the model is loaded) and store it as self.redis_client, then reuse the persistent connection in predict(). P99 latency spikes are characteristic of connection setup overhead under concurrent load. B is wrong — external dependencies in predict() are supported; the issue is initialization cost. C (async thread pool) could help throughput but complicates the synchronous pyfunc interface. D is opinionated without evidence — Redis latency when using a persistent connection is typically sub-millisecond.
questions.reference: MLflow pyfunc — initializing external connections in load_context() for reuse in predict()

questions.stem: A team deploys a new model version to a Databricks Model Serving endpoint with 5% traffic. After verifying 5% traffic for 2 days, they want to increase to 25%, then 50%, then 100% over the following week. What is this deployment pattern called?

options.text: Blue-green deployment
Shadow deployment
Canary deployment with progressive traffic ramping
Rolling deployment

questions.explanation: C is correct because a canary deployment routes a small percentage of real production traffic to the new version, monitors its behavior, and progressively increases the traffic share over time if metrics are satisfactory. The 5% → 25% → 50% → 100% ramp is the defining characteristic of a canary rollout. A (blue-green) involves an instant switchover between two full environments — no traffic splitting. B (shadow deployment) routes traffic to a new model but discards its predictions — users only see the champion's responses. D (rolling deployment) applies to stateful services where instances are updated one at a time — not a traffic splitting pattern.
questions.reference: Model Deployment — canary deployment: progressive traffic ramping pattern

questions.stem: An ML team is calling a Databricks Model Serving endpoint for batch-ish workloads from a Spark job using a foreach loop on the driver. After load testing, they find this approach has poor throughput. Select TWO better alternatives.

options.text: Switch to mlflow.pyfunc.spark_udf() to distribute inference across Spark workers without going through the REST endpoint
Use Python's concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor on the driver to send requests to the endpoint in parallel
Convert the Spark job to a Pandas pipeline and call the endpoint once with the entire DataFrame as a single request
Increase the Model Serving endpoint's provisioned concurrency to match the number of rows

questions.explanation: A is correct because spark_udf() distributes inference natively across Spark workers without HTTP overhead, making it the most efficient batch inference pattern for large Spark DataFrames. B is correct because if the REST endpoint must be used (e.g., due to feature store integration or governance requirements), parallelizing requests with a thread pool on the driver substantially improves throughput over sequential foreach by saturating the endpoint's concurrency capacity. C is wrong — most endpoints have request size limits (typically 16MB), making a single request containing an entire large DataFrame infeasible. D is wrong — increasing endpoint concurrency only helps if requests are made concurrently; sequential foreach loop cannot benefit from higher concurrency.
questions.reference: Model Serving — batch throughput: spark_udf() vs concurrent REST requests vs sequential

questions.stem: An ML engineer logs a pyfunc model that includes a custom tokenizer. At inference time, the serving container must have the 'transformers' library available. They use pip_requirements=['transformers==4.28.0'] in log_model(). At serve time, the environment has transformers==4.35.0 pre-installed. With env_manager='local', which version is used?

options.text: 4.28.0 — the requirements.txt always overrides the pre-installed version
4.35.0 — env_manager='local' uses the existing environment without installing from requirements.txt
The serving fails because the version mismatch is detected and blocked
MLflow automatically resolves the conflict by finding the latest compatible version

questions.explanation: B is correct because env_manager='local' (the default for Model Serving endpoints unless otherwise configured) uses the Python environment already available in the serving container without creating a new isolated environment or installing from requirements.txt. The pre-installed transformers==4.35.0 is what gets used. This is why version mismatches between training and serving environments can cause silent differences in behavior. A is wrong — requirements.txt is not installed with env_manager='local'. C is wrong — no automatic version conflict detection occurs with local env_manager. D is wrong — MLflow does not auto-resolve dependency conflicts.
questions.reference: MLflow Model Serving — env_manager='local' uses pre-installed environment, not requirements.txt

questions.stem: An ML model predicts customer lifetime value (CLV). The model was trained on data from Q1-Q2, but the business expanded into a new geographic market in Q3, and the new customers have very different purchasing patterns. The model's predictions for new-market customers are poor. Which type of drift best describes this?

options.text: Concept drift — the relationship P(CLV | features) is the same but the model was not trained on the new market's feature space
Data drift (covariate shift) — the new market's customer features are outside the training distribution, combined with potential concept drift for the new segment
Label drift — the CLV values themselves have changed because the new market has higher average spend
Model drift — the model weights changed due to the new market data

questions.explanation: B is correct because the new market introduces customers with feature values (geography, demographics, behavior patterns) outside the training distribution — this is covariate shift/data drift in P(X). Additionally, the relationship between features and CLV (P(Y|X)) may itself differ for new-market customers vs the original market, which is concept drift. In practice, geographic expansion often causes both simultaneously. A is wrong because the new market's feature patterns are outside the training distribution, which is data drift by definition — the model failing on new inputs due to unseen feature values. C (label drift) would refer to the marginal CLV distribution changing without feature changes. D is wrong — model weights are static after training; they do not change when new data arrives.
questions.reference: Monitoring — new market expansion causing data drift and potential concept drift

questions.stem: A monitoring team wants to compute the Jensen-Shannon divergence between the training distribution and production distribution of a model's predicted scores. They have training_probs (list of floats) and prod_probs (list of floats). Which scipy function computes this?

options.text: from scipy.stats import ks_2samp; ks_2samp(training_probs, prod_probs)
from scipy.spatial.distance import jensenshannon; jensenshannon(training_hist, prod_hist)
from scipy.stats import chi2_contingency; chi2_contingency([training_probs, prod_probs])
from scipy.stats import wasserstein_distance; wasserstein_distance(training_probs, prod_probs)

questions.explanation: B is correct because scipy.spatial.distance.jensenshannon() computes the Jensen-Shannon divergence between two probability distributions represented as histograms (discrete probability vectors). The inputs should be normalized histogram arrays (frequencies that sum to 1 for each distribution), not raw sample lists. JS divergence is bounded between 0 and 1 (when using log base 2), making it interpretable. A (ks_2samp) computes the KS statistic, not JS divergence. C (chi2_contingency) performs a chi-squared test of independence, not JS divergence. D (wasserstein_distance) computes the Earth Mover's Distance, a different distribution distance metric.
questions.reference: Drift Detection — scipy.spatial.distance.jensenshannon() for JS divergence

questions.stem: A Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring configuration uses a TIMESERIES monitor type with granularities=['1 day', '1 week'] on an inference table. What does monitoring with two granularities provide that a single daily granularity alone cannot?

options.text: Two granularities run the monitoring job twice as frequently, providing double the monitoring coverage
Weekly granularity smooths out daily noise to detect gradual long-term drift trends that may be obscured by day-to-day variability in the daily granularity
Weekly granularity uses different statistical tests than daily, providing complementary test results
Two granularities are required to enable both feature drift and model quality monitoring simultaneously

questions.explanation: B is correct because configuring multiple time granularities in Lakehouse Monitoring creates separate time-windowed statistics at each granularity. Daily metrics capture short-term fluctuations and sudden shifts, while weekly metrics aggregate across 7 days, smoothing out daily noise (e.g., weekend vs. weekday effects, short-term data pipeline issues) to reveal sustained gradual drift that individual daily windows might not clearly show. Together, daily + weekly granularity enables detection of both sudden distributional shifts and slow drift. A is wrong — the monitoring job runs on the same schedule regardless of granularity count. C is wrong — both granularities use the same statistical tests; only the window size differs. D is wrong — feature drift and model quality monitoring are enabled by monitor type (InferenceLog), not granularity count.
questions.reference: Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring — multiple granularities for multi-scale drift detection

questions.stem: An ML team monitors a customer churn model. They observe that the model's predicted churn probability for the top decile of at-risk customers has dropped from 0.87 to 0.64, while overall accuracy remains the same. Which type of drift is most specifically indicated, and what is its business implication?

options.text: Data drift in top-decile customers' features, implying the retention campaign targeting this segment may be working

Prediction drift in the high-risk segment: the model is less confident in its top at-risk predictions, potentially causing the business to under-invest in retention for customers who are still at high risk
Concept drift affecting only the top decile, indicating the model should be retrained on decile-stratified data
Label drift in the training data, which caused the model to produce lower scores

questions.explanation: B is correct because the shift in top-decile predicted churn probabilities (0.87 → 0.64) is prediction drift localized to the high-risk segment. The business implication is significant: if the model is used to target retention campaigns at customers above a probability threshold (e.g., 0.80), the drop to 0.64 means many formerly high-risk customers fall below the intervention threshold — even if they remain at high actual churn risk. This could cause the business to under-invest in retention for customers who still need intervention. A (retention campaign working) would be confirmed only by comparing to actual churn labels, not just prediction scores. C (decile-stratified retraining) is an incorrect conclusion from score drift alone. D (label drift) refers to training data, not serving-time predictions.
questions.reference: Monitoring — segment-level prediction drift and business impact on targeting thresholds

questions.stem: A team builds a scheduled Databricks Workflow that checks drift metrics from Lakehouse Monitoring and conditionally triggers retraining. Select TWO correct statements about implementing this conditional retraining architecture.

options.text: The drift-checking task can use dbutils.jobs.taskValues.set(key='retrain', value='true') to pass a flag to downstream tasks
The retraining task should use a 'Run If' condition referencing the drift flag task value to conditionally skip retraining when drift is not detected
Databricks Workflows automatically reads Lakehouse Monitoring output tables and triggers retraining without any custom task code
The retraining task must always run to update the training data, even if drift is below the threshold

questions.explanation: A is correct because dbutils.jobs.taskValues.set() is the Databricks Workflows mechanism for passing data between tasks. The drift-checking task can set a boolean flag (e.g., 'retrain'='true' or 'false') based on its analysis of the monitoring metrics table, and downstream tasks can reference this value. B is correct because Databricks Workflows' 'Run If' conditions support expressions referencing task values from upstream tasks (e.g., '{{tasks.drift_check.values.retrain}} == "true"'), enabling conditional task execution. Together, A and B implement the complete conditional retraining pattern. C is wrong — Lakehouse Monitoring only computes drift metrics; it does not automatically trigger retraining workflows. D contradicts the purpose of drift-triggered retraining, which is to retrain only when needed.
questions.reference: Databricks Workflows — taskValues and Run If for conditional retraining pipeline

questions.stem: A credit scoring model was trained on data where 5% of applicants defaulted. In production, the default rate is now 12%. The model was also built during low-interest-rate conditions; rates have since risen significantly. A data scientist checks feature drift and finds that income and employment features are stable, but interest-rate-related features show significant drift. Which combination of drift types is present?

options.text: Only data drift — the interest-rate features have shifted
Data drift in interest-rate features AND label drift (higher default rate) AND likely concept drift (rising rates change the default probability given stable income/employment)
Only concept drift — the relationship between credit features and default has changed
Prior probability shift only — the 5% to 12% default rate change is the only drift present

questions.explanation: B is correct because this scenario exhibits multiple simultaneous drift types: (1) Data drift: interest-rate-related input features have shifted significantly from training distribution — a clear covariate shift in P(X). (2) Label drift / prior probability shift: the marginal default rate P(Y) has risen from 5% to 12% — the base rate of the target has changed. (3) Concept drift: rising interest rates create a new relationship between features and default probability — even applicants with identical income and employment to the training period now face higher default risk due to higher debt service costs, changing P(Y|X). A identifies only one of the three types. C identifies only concept drift, missing the feature and label distribution shifts. D identifies only label/prior drift, missing the feature drift and the concept drift caused by the macroeconomic change.
questions.reference: Monitoring — complex drift scenarios: simultaneous data drift, label drift, and concept drift
